paper.title: When Does Combining Language Models Help? A Co-Failure Ceiling on Routing, Voting, and Mixture-of-Agents Across 67 Frontier Models

problem_reconstruction.community_question: How much accuracy gain can multi-model orchestration (routing, voting, fusion, MoA) deliver over using a single LLM?
problem_reconstruction.community_answer: It depends on the pairwise error correlation ρ. Lower ρ means more diversity, which means better ensemble accuracy.
problem_reconstruction.authors_answer: Pairwise ρ is blind to the true ceiling. The fundamental quantity is β — the rate at which ALL models fail on the same query. No selection policy can exceed 1−β, and ρ cannot identify β for m≥3 models. You must measure β directly.
problem_reconstruction.gap: The field treats ρ as a sufficient diagnostic for orchestration value. The paper shows (i) ρ is not a sufficient statistic for β (Prop. 3), (ii) the Gaussian-copula prediction from ρ systematically underprices β (Prop. 2), and (iii) the gap grows with pool size — exactly the regime where orchestration is most relevant.
problem_reconstruction.reframing: Stop asking 'how correlated are the models?' and start asking 'how often are ALL models wrong on the same question?' One is a pairwise statistic; the other is a multivariate ceiling. They are not interchangeable.

core_narrative.hook: The orchestration ceiling is not set by average pairwise agreement, but by the rare tail where every model fails together. That tail is invisible to the standard diagnostic.
escalation: Practitioners report ρ to decide whether to orchestrate. But ρ is a pairwise statistic; the ceiling is multivariate.
The Gaussian-copula model — which the field implicitly trusts — predicts β from ρ. Empirically, it fails: β is systematically higher than predicted.
The failure worsens with pool size. The very regime where orchestration seems most useful (many models) is where ρ is most misleading.
It's not just a Gaussian artifact: even a heavy-tailed Clayton copula falls short. The driver is a common-mode atom that no pairwise statistic captures.
core_narrative.resolution: Use the Clopper-Pearson certificate (Prop. 1): from a small labeled sample, get a $0 bound on maximum orchestration gain. Measure β directly, don't infer it from ρ.

force-1.name: Ceiling vs. Diagnostic Mismatch
force-1.description: The ceiling (β) is a multivariate quantity, but the field's diagnostic (ρ) is pairwise. They are not even identifiable from each other (Prop. 3).
force-1.symptom: Practitioners see low ρ and assume large orchestration headroom, when actually β may already be high.

force-2.name: Gaussian Copula Optimism
force-2.description: The single-factor Gaussian copula — the standard model for ensemble error floors — systematically underprices co-failure because it has zero lower tail dependence when ρ<1.
force-2.symptom: β predicted from ρ is 2.5× to 8.3× lower than empirical β on open-ended tasks.

force-3.name: Pool Size Amplification
force-3.description: The β/ρ gap grows with the number of models. Orchestration across large pools — the most interesting regime — is where the standard diagnostic performs worst.
force-3.symptom: Ratio rises from 1.0 at 2 models to 2.5 at 67 models on MATH-500. More models don't dilute co-failure; they reveal it.

force-4.name: Format Dictates Tail Structure
force-4.description: Multiple-choice questions suppress co-failure (β≈0). Open-ended questions amplify it. The same content in different formats produces opposite regimes.
force-4.symptom: GPQA-Diamond: β≈0 multiple-choice, β=0.127 free-response. Format, not subject, sets the regime.

force-5.name: Quality Diversity Trade-off
force-5.description: Diverse models help only when quality is matched. Naive heterogeneous fusion (unequal-quality models) hurts: it lets weaker members outvote a strong one.
force-5.symptom: 455 triplets: mean vote gain negative (−0.10). At matched quality: lower ρ helps (+0.027 gain, positive in all 60 resamples).

force-6.name: Realizability Gap
force-6.description: Even when the ceiling is open (β≈0), deployable routers capture almost none of the oracle gain. The signal for which model will be correct on a given query is not in the prompt.
force-6.symptom: TF-IDF+domain router: 9% of G (CI spans 0). LLM-as-router: 0%. The oracle G=0.154 on GPQA is pure resolvable disagreement no router harvests.

entities.description: Pairwise error correlation — the standard diagnostic
Gaussian copula with single common factor
Gain a deployable router actually achieves
Statistical bound on maximum orchestration gain from labeled sample
Cascade reduces to random mixing as verifier AUC → ½
Dual price on the inference dollar in budget-constrained routing

relationships.evidence: Not copula misspecification; survives Clayton copula control

key_insight.text: The co-failure tail β, not pairwise correlation ρ, is the right quantity for orchestration decisions. The standard diagnostic (ρ) is blind to the ceiling, the gap grows with pool size, and a simple Clopper-Pearson certificate from a small labeled sample directly measures what matters.
key_insight.unifying_principle: Orchestration is allocation over a priced, correlated, churning pool. All three orchestration modes (routing, fusion, cascade) are governed by the same ceiling 1−β because all output member-model answers. The correct economic framing replaces the pair-correlation heuristic with a multivariate ceiling and a direct measurement tool.

strengths: 67-model pool across 21 providers is genuinely market-scale
Content-controlled test (GPQA MC vs. free-response) rules out domain confound
Proper tetrachoric calibration (avoids the common Pearson trap)
Full 67×67 Gaussian copula Monte Carlo (not just single-factor)
Clayton copula control (non-Gaussian tail dependence ruled out)
Pool-size ablation (60 random k-subsets per k, monotonic rise confirmed)
weaknesses: Programmatic grading only covers verifiable tasks — limits to tasks with known answer
Code domain: k=5 all-wrong events, strict-but-not-official judge, 18-model pool (not 67)
Unconditional ρ-slope inference inconclusive under model-clustering
G and block-ρ gap reported without seed replication
Matched-quality test rests on one provider-matched band; alternative pipeline gives smaller non-significant gain
Cascade verifier scores only cheap model L (not both L and H as optimal deferral requires)
Churn study (App. E) is observational and stylized, not controlled

boundary_conditions: Open-ended generative tasks beyond verifiable benchmarks (not tested)

future_work_needed: Tight-ratio code replication at 67-model scale with official judge
≥3 seeds and held-out model selection on matched-quality result
Extension across multiple ρ levels to fit λ and predict k* out-of-sample
Human-calibrated judge on full 130 GPQA open-ended questions
Open-ended generative tasks beyond verifiable benchmarks

impact_assessment.immediate_practical_value: The Clopper-Pearson certificate (Prop. 1) is a $0 pre-deployment test any team can run: from a small labeled sample, get a certified upper bound on the gain any router/vote/cascade can deliver. If the bound falls below overhead cost, don't build orchestration.
impact_assessment.theoretical_impact: Shifts the frame from pairwise to multivariate, from ρ to β. Connects LLM orchestration to portfolio theory (Markowitz), copula credit modeling (CDO base correlation smile), and common-shock models (Marshall-Olkin).
impact_assessment.methodological_impact: Exposes the Pearson-tetrachoric calibration trap that the LLM evaluation community routinely falls into. A century-old psychometric correction is load-bearing.
impact_assessment.pipeline_impact: If correct, the ceiling-bound regime (open-ended tasks) means that on the workloads where orchestration would be most valuable, it's least possible. The realizable headroom is small, and the signal for query-level routing is not in the prompt.
open_questions: Does the co-failure signature hold on truly open-ended generative tasks (summarization, creative writing, analysis)?
Can a stronger query-level routing signal (e.g. embedding-based, learned on per-model performance prediction) realize the oracle gain on realizability-bound tasks?
Does the common-mode atom shrink or persist as frontier models improve?
